So what's a built-in module? In short, it's a module that comes built into Node.js — you don't need to install anything extra to use it. These modules are provided by Node.js itself, so we can use them directly. That's what "built-in module" means.
In the example above, we used the
built-in module. Through this module, we can quickly build a simple server. Since this module is from Node.js itself, we can use it directly.

But because JavaScript file handling is asynchronous, we have to use

Callback Hell makes code overly nested and hard to read. So Node.js 10.0.0 added the
module. Taking the read-file example, we can rewrite it like this:

The Callback concept is passing a function as a parameter to another function. When something is done, we run the function — that function is the callback. Callback Hell is the nested structure of callbacks, which makes code hard to read.

is mainly for encryption and decryption. The most common scenario is during user registration — we encrypt the user's password so it doesn't leak. When the user logs in, we encrypt the input password and compare against the database to confirm the input is correct.
provides many methods — sha256, sha512, md5, etc. Here's a simple example:

In short, we used sha256 to hash the user's password into a string of garbled characters — preventing the password from leaking. What does this mean? When we develop systems, we always need to record user accounts and passwords. But for security, we don't store passwords in plaintext — we hash them. That way the password won't leak, since even system admins can't see it.
Plaintext means a password without any processing — e.g., if the password is
But sha256 is just basic — usually you also use Salt (salting). That's not the focus here, so I won't go into it.
Back to sha256 — once we've hashed with sha256, how do we compare during login? Just hash the password the user inputs, then compare with the hashed password in the DB:
// Assume this is the stored user password hash in the database
// Compare the hashed user input with the stored hash from the database
Pretty simple right? But this is just a basic example — in practice you also want to use Salt, which gives much better protection against password leaks (this is important).
I want to wrap up here — Node.js has too many built-in modules. Going through them all would be a lot, QQ.
So this post mainly covers a few common ones. The rest you can use based on your dev needs.

cache mechanism. When modules are imported, they're cached (in

) to avoid re-importing. So when we import a module, Node.js first checks if the module is cached — if so, it uses the cached one; if not, it loads the module fresh. This significantly improves performance, avoids re-importing, and allows shared state.
prefix was introduced around Node.js 14 (I think?). When you use the
cache mechanism. What does this mean? Even if the module exists in
, it's ignored — Node.js loads the module fresh. This makes sure you're using the latest module instead of the cached one.
